pages.name: Site Overview — What Tarnsmere Tollhouse Really Was

text.content:
Site Overview — Tarnsmere Tollhouse

The truth (GM only)

  • Tarnsmere Tollhouse began as a surface mining toll and survey station, around 410 CY, serving a profitable iron-and-tin claim on what would later be called the Tarnsmere Spur.
  • Within fifty years, it had become something else: a controlled access point to an older descent route already in use by the deeper faction.
  • The route was administered through the Aerdy Permissive Survey's Class VII classification (see Phase 7 — Iren's Glossary). The tollhouse's surface paperwork was an administrative cover for the descent permission system.
  • When the Aerdy Permissive Survey was dissolved in 542 CY, the route was erased from public maps and guild records. The tollhouse was administratively closed as “unprofitable.”
  • The tollhouse records were not financial; they tracked who had been admitted to descend. Every line entry was a person's name, the date of admission, and (when applicable) a return date that almost never came.
  • The Seven-Cut Cairn is the final surface waymark. It is not the entrance.
  • The hidden stairhouse beneath the tollhouse hearth is the true threshold.
  • Recent conspiracy activity has reactivated the route — route-cleaners have been refreshing wax seals, repairing the Permission Door, and quietly removing surface evidence that the tollhouse was ever more than a ruin.

What the party can learn

  • The descent was controlled by tokens, seals, passphrases, or recognised marks.
  • The route predates the current Blooming Rot operation by at least a century. The conspiracy did not build it; they rented it.
  • The conspiracy uses old infrastructure rather than building new routes.
  • Haskur was being handed off, not travelling freely. The descent was scheduled.
  • Kethren's role as route courier matters because he knows the formal protocol for being admitted.

The six surface locations

The site has six surface locations the party can investigate. They can be visited in any order. Most reward careful examination over force.

  1. Old Mining Spur Road — the approach.
  2. Seven-Cut Cairn — the final waymark.
  3. Collapsed Survey Station — broken civic ruin downslope.
  4. Ruined Tarnsmere Tollhouse — main ruin, hides the stairhouse entrance.
  5. Dry Well — not a well; ventilation shaft for the stairhouse.
  6. Tollhouse Yard — open ground for confrontation or watching.

Location 1 — Old Mining Spur Road

The approach. Weathered ruts, grass-grown track, scattered stone chips. The road has been formally abandoned for thirty years but shows too much use for that to be the whole truth.

Clues

  • Recent narrow bootprints — not boot styles the party has seen. Soft soles, narrow last, made within the last two weeks.
  • Cart tracks that do not match normal wagons. Narrower wheelbase. Two-wheel only, suggesting hand-carts or pack-mules with sledges.
  • One flake of dark wax caught in a wheel rut. Matches Aldea's unknown recipe-family.
  • Old survey nails in stones along the road verge — iron pins driven by a Yeomanry surveyor decades ago to mark the road as official. They have not been removed by anyone authorised. Someone has been maintaining the road on the quiet.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Survival13Recent passage; bootprints two weeks old or less.
Investigation14The road was maintained longer than records suggest. The grass has been cut, not just trampled.
History15Connects road style to old Aerdy mining claims; the road predates the Yeomanry's claim to the region.

Fail-forward

Even with all checks failed, the party can see the bootprints and the wheel ruts. They will not draw the right conclusions yet, but they will know someone has been here.

Gianni's thread

If Gianni's geographic-erasure thread is active: she identifies the road as the same one that should appear on her copy of the old Aerdi survey map (which she may have gotten from Lt. Korven Mada in Phase 4). The road is not on the current map. She has the proof of one of the four missing spurs in her hands.

Location 2 — Seven-Cut Cairn (revisited from Phase 7)

The party reached the cairn at the end of Phase 7. Phase 8 returns to it with new questions. The cairn is no longer just a marker; it is now evidence.

What the party should re-examine

  • The seven cuts. Are they all still present? If Lockdown Clock 1+, the seventh has been scraped.
  • The blackened hill mark. The blackening is old mineral darkening, not paint or magic.
  • The base of the cairn. A wedge stone at the cairn's south face is loose; behind it, in a crevice, is a small offering — a copper toll-token from at least sixty years ago, left by someone who descended and did not come back. Investigation DC 13 to find.
  • The view from the cairn. The cairn's east face is aligned with the tollhouse hearth. Standing at the cairn at sunrise, the sunrise line passes directly over the hearth. This is not coincidence. Wisdom (Survival) DC 14 to notice, or History DC 14 if the party has been studying mining-charter conventions.

Clues if the clock is high

  • The seventh cut has been scraped almost flat. The blackening on the hill mark is partly chipped away. The damage is recent — within the last 24–48 hours.
  • Fresh chisel marks at the base of the cairn show the cleaner's working position.
  • A small grey-and-bone thread caught on a sharp stone edge — route-cleaner uniform.

The wedge stone offering

The toll-token recovered from the cairn crevice is small, copper, worn nearly smooth. On one face: a single descending step. On the other: the worn outline of what might once have been a name. This is a real Tarnsmere descent token, sixty or more years old. It is functional for opening the Permission Door, but it is also fragile (Strength save DC 10 if dropped on stone; otherwise treat as a usable token).

Selvara's thread

If Selvara's dreams-of-cut-stone thread is active: she now recognises the cairn's seventh cut exactly. She has seen this mark in dreams since Phase 1. The recognition is not magical. It is inherited.

Location 3 — Collapsed Survey Station

A small stone building, half roofless, downslope from the tollhouse. About 15 feet by 20 feet, single room. The walls are intact to chest height; the roof has fallen in on the east side. The interior is half-shielded from rain by the surviving west wall.

What's inside

  • A broken map table — oak, the legs cracked, the top splintered. Once held a survey of the local mining claim. Most of the table has been ripped up for firewood by passing travellers over the years.
  • Ruined measuring rods — brass-tipped, broken, scattered. A surveyor's compass with no glass.
  • Old ledger scraps — pages torn from a bound book that no longer exists. Most are unreadable. A few legible fragments survive.
  • A small iron strongbox — rusted shut. Empty. Once held survey fees.

Clues

  • A survey map fragment shows a tunnel that is no longer on any modern map — one shaft scratched out forcefully, with the same iron-gall ink used by Yeomanry surveyors. This is a Garrold Eft scratchout if Gianni's thread is active.
  • One ledger fragment shows initials matching a Guild record from Phase 7 — the same hand that wrote the Tarnsmere Spur claim record. This is the work of the missing 542 CY surveyor.
  • A recent footprint over old dust, just inside the surviving doorway. Someone has been here in the last week.
  • A burned scrap in the cold hearth, almost entirely charred but still legible at one corner: “permission not passage”.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation15Recovers the ledger scrap with the matching initials.
Investigation13Notices the recent footprint and identifies it as a route-cleaner's narrow boot.
History14The survey map fragment confirms a closed mining claim.

Hazard — unstable roof stones

The east wall is bowed inward. Heavy footfalls, magic discharge, or careless climbing on the wall can dislodge stones. Dexterity save DC 13 if disturbed; failure causes 1d6 bludgeoning damage and prone. Mason's tools, mining knowledge, or careful stone-bracing can stabilise the roof and remove the hazard.

Gianni's thread

If Gianni examines the scratched-out tunnel on the survey map: the scratchout is Garrold Eft's work. The hand matches the journal page from the Phase 7 Survey Hut vignette. Eft was here. He scratched out the route to warn future surveyors. He did not return to remove the map entirely. He did not have time.

Location 4 — Ruined Tarnsmere Tollhouse

The main ruin. About 30 feet by 20 feet, single storey originally with a small upper room since collapsed. Stone walls, broken toll window, cracked hearth, the upper floor's timbers come down through what's left of the roof.

Layout

  • Main room (front): toll counter at the window, broken; office area behind the counter; broken chairs and a fallen shelf.
  • Hearth wall (rear): a large stone hearth, blackened, with a flat hearth-plate set into the floor in front of it. The hearth is the entry to the stairhouse.
  • Side storage: a small alcove room, mostly intact, where toll-tokens and registry books were once kept. Has its own locked door (DC 14).
  • Upper room remains: a half-collapsed loft. Climbable (DC 12 Athletics) for a high observation point. Nothing valuable up there now.

Civic surface details

  • The toll window faces the wrong road angle. A surveyor would notice immediately (Survival DC 13 or Investigation DC 13): the window is set to look down the mining spur road as it approaches the cairn, not as it leaves the tollhouse toward the city. The tollhouse was watching for arrivals from below, not from above.
  • Interior wall has hidden tally cuts. Inside the office area, behind where a coat-rack used to stand, a vertical row of small cuts has been made into the plaster: not a count, a record of admissions. Investigation DC 14 to find. There are seventeen cuts. The bottom three are deeper than the rest — recent.
  • Hearth stone conceals a stairhouse key-slot or route-token socket. The hearth plate at the foot of the hearth can be lifted by inserting a Tarnsmere descent token into a recessed slot, or by careful prying with thieves' tools, or by force.
  • Old registry chain hangs from a hook above the office desk. The chain once secured a bound book to the desk. The book is gone; the chain remains.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation14Finds the hidden tally cuts inside the office wall.
Perception15Hears a faint cold draft from below the hearth plate.
Thieves' tools15Opens the hearth plate cleanly. No noise. No Structural Failure advance.
Strength (force the plate)18Forces it, but advances Structural Failure Clock by 1 and produces a loud crash.
Investigation (toll-window angle)13Confirms the tollhouse was watching for arrivals from below.

The hearth plate

The hearth plate is the entry point. It is approximately 4 feet square, made of fitted stone, set flush with the floor. Around its edge, just visible if the dust is brushed away, are seven shallow channel-marks. If a Tarnsmere descent token is placed in the central recess, the plate sinks two inches and slides aside, revealing the narrow stone stair below.

If the party does not have a token, they can pry, force, or finesse the plate open by other means. This is the first reminder that the site responds to credentials.

Location 5 — Dry Well

What looks like a well beside the tollhouse, 30 feet east of the rear wall. Stone-collared, about 4 feet across, with the remains of a wooden cover. The cover is half-rotted. The well has no bucket and no rope. It does have iron rungs set into the stone wall of the shaft.

The truth

This is not a well. It is a ventilation shaft for the stairhouse below. It also functions as an alternate entry for small or determined characters.

Clues

  • Cold upward draft. The shaft moves air constantly. The temperature at the lip is several degrees colder than the surrounding ground.
  • Old iron rungs too narrow for buckets. Each rung is hand-sized, set for climbing, not for hoisting water. The well-collar lacks any pulley attachment points.
  • Soot marks from recent lamp use on the upper rungs. Someone has climbed up or down within the last week, carrying a small lamp.
  • Faint echo of water from below — not in the shaft itself, but somewhere connected to the bottom of it. The cistern drain runs near the shaft's base.

The shaft

  • Depth: 35 feet.
  • Width: 4 feet at top, narrowing to about 3 feet at the bottom.
  • Rungs: iron, intact, set every 2 feet.
  • Bottom: opens into a horizontal stone passage that leads to the cistern drain (Subsurface Location 11) and, by a longer detour, to the old guardroom (Subsurface Location 10).

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Athletics (climb down)14Reaches the bottom safely. Failure: 1d6 falling damage per 10 ft of fall.
Perception13Hears movement below if route-cleaners are using the cistern drain as an escape route.
Survival13Identifies the draft direction as constant and source-deep: ventilation, not a normal well.
Stealth (climbing without sound)13Reaches the bottom without alerting anyone below.

Use

The dry well provides:

  • Alternate entry for the subsurface threshold, bypassing the tollhouse hearth.
  • Dangerous shortcut for small or agile characters who want to scout ahead.
  • Route-cleaner escape route if the party engages cleaners in the tollhouse — the cleaners may try to flee down the well to escape via the cistern drain.

Cam, with halfling proportions, can climb the narrow lower section more easily than the human PCs. The well is functional for the whole party but rewards small-bodied scouting.

Location 6 — Tollhouse Yard

The open area in front of the tollhouse, where carts and pack-mules once stopped to pay their toll. Now: broken posts, old hitching rings rusted in place, cracked paving stones, weeds in the cracks. About 40 feet by 30 feet, bounded by the tollhouse, the road, and a low stone wall along the east edge.

This is where confrontations happen

The yard is the most likely place for the party to encounter route-cleaner agents outside the threshold complex. Three encounter triggers:

A. Surface watcher (Lockdown Clock 1+)

A silent scout perched on the ridge above the yard, watching the party arrive. If spotted (Passive Perception 14), the scout will not engage; he will flee on foot toward the road and eventually toward Greyhawk to report. The party can chase or let him go. Letting him go advances the Lockdown Clock by 1.

B. Tollhouse erase-team (Lockdown Clock 3+)

Two route-cleaner agents are inside the tollhouse burning registry scraps when the party arrives. The party can:

  • Burst in and engage. The agents will fight briefly then flee toward the dry well.
  • Sneak in. With careful Stealth (DC 14 group), the party can observe the agents and intervene at a moment of advantage.
  • Wait outside. The agents will leave within an hour; the party can search the tollhouse after, recovering at most half the registry fragments that survived.

C. Local scavenger or ridge watcher (always)

An unrelated local figure may pass through during the party's investigation:

  • A charcoal-burner, looking for sites to harvest the next season. Friendly, unhelpful, will leave when asked.
  • A shepherd's child looking for a strayed lamb. Frightened. Asks if the party has seen the lamb. (They have not.) She is the only friendly local encounter on site.
  • A retired Yeomanry surveyor — not Dalmor Vetch — passing through on a private errand. Has heard rumours of strange activity on the spur. Will report what he sees to Vetch later. Useful only if the party engages him politely.

Clues on the yard's stones

  • One fresh wheel rut across the dust at the yard's southern edge — a narrow hand-cart, made within the last three days.
  • Spilled grey sand in a small pile near the tollhouse entrance — sand from a sand-box used to soak up wax during sealing work. Route-cleaners have been resealing the Permission Door.
  • If the Lockdown Clock is 3+: a small bloodstain on a paving stone near the dry well, where a cleaner sliced his hand on a chisel and bled briefly before bandaging.
  • If a captured agent's fled here recently: a black thread caught on a broken hitching ring, snagged from a grey-and-bone uniform.

Visibility

The yard is exposed. Cover is limited to the tollhouse walls (full cover), the broken posts (half cover), and the low stone wall (half cover for crouched figures). Any combat in the yard is visible from the ridge above, which means any silent scout watching the site can report on what happened.
